title: Invasive non-typhoidal salmonella death rate in children under five
subtitle: Estimated annual deaths in children under five from [invasive non-typhoidal salmonella](#dod:non-typhoidal-salmonella), per 100,000 people in the same age group.
caption:
Invasive non-typhoidal salmonella death rate in children under five

Estimated annual deaths in children under five from invasive non-typhoidal salmonella, per 100,000 people in the same age group.

This choropleth world map displays 2023 estimated annual death rates from invasive non-typhoidal salmonella in children under five, per 100,000 children in that age group. The legend ranges from 0 to over 100, with labeled thresholds at 0, 1, 2, 5, 10, 20, 50, and 100, plus a “No data” category shown with hatching. The darkest purple shades — indicating rates above 50 and extending into the 100+ band — are concentrated in West Africa: Mali, Burkina Faso, Niger, and Nigeria fall clearly within this highest visible range. Surrounding countries including Senegal, Guinea, Ivory Coast, Ghana, Cameroon, and Chad appear in mid-to-dark purple or blue-purple tones, placing them between the 10 and 50 thresholds. A broader swath of Sub-Saharan Africa, including Ethiopia, Kenya, Tanzania, Zambia, and South Africa, shows lighter blue shades corresponding to values between 1 and 10. Most of Asia, Latin America, Europe, North America, and Australia sit in the lightest shades, near or below 1. Greenland and several small island territories show either very pale coloring or hatching; their exact status is indistinct but they do not exhibit the dark bands seen in West Africa. The visual pattern centers elevated death rates in a contiguous zone across West and Central Africa, with progressively lighter shades radiating outward to other regions. No country outside Africa appears in the darkest bands. The map’s color gradient emphasizes regional clustering rather than isolated outliers, with the highest values confined to a specific geographic corridor.
